current.model: manual (curated from a supplied set of saved postings)
current.overview: Across 22 recent CTI, detection engineering, and threat hunting postings — from frontier AI labs (Anthropic, OpenAI) through security vendors (CrowdStrike, Palo Alto, Cloudflare, Group-IB, TENEX) to banks and consultancies (BofA, Citi, EY, Deloitte) — employers want analysts who can run the full intelligence lifecycle AND operationalize it: turning actor research into detections, hunts, and disruption rather than reports that get filed. Python/SQL scripting is now effectively table stakes at every level, and AI/LLM-assisted investigation is the clearest emerging differentiator, appearing in roughly half the postings.
top_skills: Threat actor and TTP tracking mapped to MITRE ATT&CK — requested in nearly every posting
Full intelligence lifecycle execution (planning, collection, analysis, production, dissemination) — explicit in about half
Hypothesis-driven threat hunting across SIEM, EDR, and cloud telemetry
OSINT tradecraft including infrastructure pivoting: WHOIS, passive DNS, certificate transparency, domain registration patterns
Operationalizing intelligence into detections, blocklists, and hunt packages — the intel-to-detection bridge is the single most repeated theme
IOC collection, validation, and enrichment with an emphasis on low false-positive, high-fidelity output
Malware triage literacy: reading sandbox reports and extracting C2/indicators (deeper static/dynamic analysis at senior levels)
Dark web and underground-ecosystem monitoring with actor profiling
Incident response support and intrusion analysis at all stages of the kill chain
Written intelligence reporting for both technical and executive audiences — universal, often called out as the deciding skill
tools_and_technologies: Python — required or strongly preferred in roughly three-quarters of postings; SQL for querying large datasets in about a third
Frameworks: MITRE ATT&CK (dominant), Cyber Kill Chain, Diamond Model, Pyramid of Pain
Threat Intelligence Platforms: OpenCTI, MISP, ThreatConnect, Recorded Future, Intel 471, Flashpoint, Mandiant Advantage
SIEM/EDR stacks: Splunk, Google SecOps/Chronicle, enterprise EDR; SOAR playbook automation appears in several
Enrichment and OSINT services: VirusTotal, Shodan, urlscan, Censys, RiskIQ, Maltego, i2 Analyst's Notebook
Detection languages: YARA, Sigma, Snort/Suricata, SIEM-native query languages
STIX/TAXII sharing standards for intelligence exchange
Cloud telemetry fluency: AWS most cited, then GCP/Azure; Terraform/IaC at engineer-tier roles
Data engineering: APIs, pipelines (Airflow, DBT), web scraping, notebooks/Pandas for analysis at scale
AI tooling: LLM-assisted analysis, prompt engineering, AI coding assistants (Claude, Cursor) named explicitly
certifications: Certifications are mostly 'one or more of the following' preferences, not hard gates — experience substitutes almost everywhere
GIAC family leads: GCTI is the most CTI-specific and most cited; also GCIH, GCIA, GCFA/GCFE, GNFA, GPEN
CISSP and Security+ appear as broad baselines; OSCP and CEH for offense-leaning roles
Niche mentions: CREST CCTIM, CPTIA/CRTIA, BTL1, Recorded Future Certified Analyst
Clearances matter for fed-adjacent work: US citizenship (Amazon), active TS/SCI (Deloitte), Top Secret listed as a plus (Anthropic, 10a Labs insider-threat check)
experience: Sweet spot: 5+ years in security with 2-4 years specifically in CTI/hunting — the most common ask at mid/senior level
A real entry path exists: 1-3 years accepted at investigator/analyst tier (10a Labs, Accenture Federal, TENEX analyst role)
SOC, IR, and detection engineering backgrounds are explicitly accepted feeders — 'or closely related role' appears in most postings
Bachelor's in CS/cybersecurity is almost always waivable via 'or equivalent experience'
Senior roles add mentoring, program building, and executive stakeholder communication
Observed compensation: ~$80-105K entry, ~$120-200K typical mid/senior, $230-405K at frontier AI labs
emerging_trends: AI on both sides of the desk: using LLMs to accelerate investigations AND investigating AI misuse itself (OpenAI, Anthropic, 10a Labs, CrowdStrike, Unit 42 all cite it)
CTI-as-engineering: 'builder' roles wanting production-quality Python, pipelines, and tooling — not just analysis (Anthropic, Cloudflare, Miro)
Intel-to-detection convergence: postings repeatedly ask for intelligence turned into YARA/Sigma/SIEM detections and measurable outcomes
Cloud and SaaS telemetry (AWS, identity, application logs) displacing pure network-centric analysis
Frontier AI labs have become a distinct, top-paying employer class for CTI talent
Machine learning literacy creeping into TI engineer roles (Cloudflare asks for the full ML lifecycle)
At least one posting embedded a hidden instruction aimed at AI screening tools — employers are actively testing for AI-generated applications, so keep application materials personally written
soft_skills: Clear writing is THE universal requirement — every single posting asks for reporting or briefing skill
Translating technical threats into business risk for executives and non-technical stakeholders
Cross-functional collaboration with SOC, IR, detection engineering, fraud, and product teams
Self-direction and comfort with ambiguity — 'minimal oversight' and 'fast-moving' recur constantly
Curiosity and continuous learning: following researchers, staying current on zero-days, wanting to know who is behind an IP and why
Calm, methodical execution during live incidents; several roles carry on-call or occasional off-hours expectations
